title: Listening - Part 4: Remote Working Experiences
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five professionals discussing the transition to working remotely full-time

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d been commuting three hours a day for ten years, and [21]the main reason I pushed for a remote role was simply to reclaim that time for my own well-being[/21]. I was exhausted. [26]The most unexpected benefit has been the massive improvement in my ability to focus on complex tasks[/26]. Without the constant interruptions of an open-plan office, my productivity has soared. I can finish in six hours what used to take me eight. It’s given me a much better work-life balance and allowed me to actually start exercising in the mornings, which has been great for my energy levels. || Speaker 2: I’m a software developer, and [22]I moved to a remote role because I wanted the freedom to live in a rural area that was far from our city headquarters[/22]. I wanted to be closer to nature. It’s been wonderful, but [27]the biggest challenge has been the profound sense of professional isolation I feel[/27]. I miss the casual whiteboard sessions and the ability to just bounce ideas off my colleagues. Slack and Zoom are fine for logistics, but they don't capture that spontaneous creative energy. I’ve had to make a real effort to attend industry meetups just to feel connected to my field. || Speaker 3: I recently became a parent, and [23]I transitioned to remote work primarily because I wanted to be more present for my child's early years[/23]. I didn't want to miss anything. It’s been a joy, but [28]I was surprised by how difficult it is to set clear boundaries between my 'work' and 'home' life[/28]. Because my laptop is always there on the kitchen table, I find myself checking emails at 9 PM. It’s hard to mentally switch off when your office is also your living room. I’ve had to create a very strict schedule and a dedicated workspace to stop work from bleeding into my family time. || Speaker 4: I’m a freelancer, and [24]I chose a fully remote lifestyle because I was tired of the high costs of renting an office space in the city[/24]. It was a purely financial decision. [29]The most rewarding outcome has been the discovery of a vibrant local community of other remote workers in my neighborhood[/29]. We meet up at a local cafe once a week to work together. It’s a whole new social circle that I never would have found if I was still commuting to the city center. It’s made my local area feel much more like a community rather than just a place where I sleep. || Speaker 5: I’d always struggled with the rigid 9-to-5 schedule, so [25]I moved to a remote company because I wanted the autonomy to work during my most productive hours[/25], which are usually late at night. I wanted more control over my day. [30]What I value most now is the complete lack of office politics and 'performative' work[/30]. I no longer have to look busy just for the sake of it. I’m judged purely on my output, which is incredibly liberating. It’s reduced my stress levels significantly and made me much more satisfied with my career overall. I feel like a professional adult for the first time.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) each speaker's main reason for choosing remote work.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted to reclaim the time lost to a long commute for her well-being.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted the freedom to live in a rural area far from headquarters.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to be more present for her child's early years.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 was tired of the high costs of renting a city office.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted the autonomy to work during her most productive hours.

options.C: to avoid a conflict with a supervisor
options.D: to travel while maintaining an income
options.E: to eliminate a lengthy daily journey
options.F: to recover from a recent burnout
options.G: to have greater flexibility in their schedule
options.H: to facilitate a change in their living environment

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of the change.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a massive improvement in her ability to focus on complex tasks.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 feels a profound sense of professional isolation.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 struggled to set clear boundaries between work and home life.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 discovered a vibrant local community of other remote workers.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 values the lack of office politics and being judged only on output.

options.A: the difficulty of missing peer interaction
options.B: a sense of being overlooked for promotion
options.C: an increase in their monthly electricity bills
options.D: the satisfaction of being evaluated on results
options.E: the pleasure of working in their pajamas
options.F: a significant boost in their concentration
options.G: the struggle to disconnect from their duties
options.H: the development of local social connections
